ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for fish head milky soup noodle. FOODON_03311737 (fish soup) is the closest defensible parent. The noodle component and milky broth are not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish soup) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for fish head milky soup noodle in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for fish head milky soup noodle as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 500 g, comprising a bowl of milky fish broth with a portion of rice vermicelli, fish head pieces, tofu, salted vegetables, and tomato slices.

nutrient_source.title: Fish Head Bee Hoon (Milky Fish Head Soup with Rice Vermicelli)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) — Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists 'Fish Head Bee Hoon' as a standard Singaporean dish. Nutrient values per 100 g are adapted from the HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food database entry for fish head bee hoon (milky soup variant). The dish served at Hai Kah Lang is consistent with this standard preparation. Values reflect the composite dish including broth, rice vermicelli, fish, tofu, and vegetables.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Rice vermicelli / rice noodles (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish head milky soup noodle as a composite dish. The GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI approximately 53, low category), which constitutes the primary glycemic carbohydrate source in this dish. The milky broth, fish, tofu, and vegetables contribute protein and fat that further moderate postprandial glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (53 × 31.3) / 100 ≈ 16.6, where carbohydrate per 500 g serving = 6.3 × 5 = 31.5 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salted vegetables, broth, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-salt preparation, as a 500 g serving provides approximately 1550 mg sodium, representing over 67% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrates, partially offset by protein and fat in broth
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in moderation; the low GI of the rice vermicelli (GI ~53) and the protein and fat content of the milky fish broth help moderate postprandial blood glucose, but the glycemic load per serving of approximately 16.6 warrants portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from evaporated milk and fish head
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the high sodium content and moderate saturated fat (approximately 3.5 g per serving) from the milky broth; opting for a smaller portion or requesting less evaporated milk can reduce cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Head Milk Soup Noodle (奶汤鱼头米粉; also known as Fish Head Bee Hoon) is a beloved Singaporean Chinese hawker and restaurant dish consisting of a rich, opaque milky broth made by simmering a fish head — typically snakehead (haruan) or grouper — with ginger, spring onion, and sometimes evaporated milk or fresh milk to achieve a characteristic creamy white colour. The broth is served over or alongside rice vermicelli (bee hoon / mee hoon), accompanied by silken tofu, salted vegetables (kiam chye), tomato slices, and garnished with fried shallots and white pepper. The dish is prized for its delicate, mildly sweet and savoury flavour, the gelatinous texture of the fish head cheeks and collar, and the comforting warmth of the milky soup. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate protein from the fish (approximately 5.2 g per 100 g), low to moderate fat (2.1 g per 100 g), and carbohydrates primarily from the rice vermicelli (6.3 g per 100 g). The sodium content is notably high due to salted vegetables and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index of the rice vermicelli component is approximately 53 (low category), and the overall glycemic load per standard 500 g serving is approximately 16.6, placing it in the moderate range for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is halal-friendly and gluten-free when prepared with rice vermicelli. It is commonly served as a lunch or dinner main course in Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops, and seafood restaurants, and is culturally associated with Teochew and Cantonese Chinese culinary traditions in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Fish Head Bee Hoon, which is the closest approved primary source for this Singaporean dish. The dish served at Hai Kah Lang is consistent with the standard preparation profile. GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component using the University of Sydney GI Database, as no direct composite-dish GI measurement exists. Confidence is medium rather than high because the nutrient values are adapted rather than an exact match to this specific restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo 'Fish Head Bee Hoon' (milky soup variant) per 100 g of the composite dish as served. Minor variation is expected across restaurants depending on the ratio of broth to noodles and the amount of evaporated milk used. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish. 5. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is a confirmed trace/negligible value consistent with the HPB source and the absence of partially hydrogenated oils in the standard preparation. 6. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on boiled rice vermicelli from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for the composite dish exists in approved sources. 7. The food_name in the input JSON ('奶汤鱼头米 Fish Head Milk Soup Noodle') uses '米' (rice/grain) rather than '米粉' (rice vermicelli); the canonical Chinese name has been corrected to '奶汤鱼头米粉' to accurately reflect the noodle dish. 8. Halal status is assessed as likely true for standard preparations using fish and dairy without pork-derived ingredients, but individual restaurant certification should be verified. 9. Gluten-free status applies when rice vermicelli is used; wheat noodle substitutions would introduce gluten.
